name: Timeline Split
description: The concept of a 'Timeline Split' posits that the linear progression of time can diverge, creating alternate realities or parallel universes stemming from pivotal decisions or events. This idea, popularized in science fiction, has also gained traction within fringe theories and some interpretations of quantum mechanics. Proponents suggest that significant historical events, quantum phenomena, or even individual choices can act as branching points, leading to the formation of distinct timelines. Evidence cited often includes the Mandela Effect (shared false memories), unexplained anomalies in historical records, and interpretations of quantum superposition and entanglement. Some proponents even believe that CERN or other high-energy physics experiments could inadvertently trigger timeline splits. Mainstream science largely dismisses timeline splits as speculative and lacking empirical evidence. The Many-Worlds Interpretation of quantum mechanics, while proposing multiple universes, differs significantly from the popular notion of timeline splits triggered by macroscopic events. This interpretation suggests that all quantum possibilities are realized in branching universes, not necessarily tied to conscious decisions or historical turning points. Critics also point to the logical paradoxes inherent in timeline split scenarios, such as the grandfather paradox, and the lack of any mechanism to detect or interact with alternate timelines.
subtitle: Divergence of reality into alternate paths

claims.claim: Significant events cause reality to fork, creating alternate timelines.
claims.evidence: Mandela Effect, historical anomalies, quantum interpretations.
claims.criticism: Lack of empirical evidence, logical paradoxes, misinterpretation of quantum mechanics.

claims.claim: High-energy physics experiments can trigger or interact with timeline splits.
claims.evidence: Speculative interpretations of CERN experiments, anecdotal claims.
claims.criticism: No scientific basis, misunderstanding of particle physics, conspiracy theories.

claims.claim: Individuals can shift between timelines through consciousness or specific techniques.
claims.evidence: Anecdotal accounts, personal experiences, new age beliefs.
claims.criticism: Subjective experiences, lack of verifiable evidence, pseudoscience.

keyFigures.name: Hugh Everett III
keyFigures.role: Proposed the Many-Worlds Interpretation of Quantum Mechanics

keyFigures.name: Michio Kaku
keyFigures.role: Popular Science communicator who discusses multiverse possibilities.

keyFigures.name: Sean Carroll
keyFigures.role: Physicist critical of easy multiverse interpretations.

timeline.event: Hugh Everett III proposes the Many-Worlds Interpretation of quantum mechanics.
Science fiction begins popularizing the concept of diverging timelines.
The Mandela Effect gains widespread attention, fueling timeline split theories.
